title: Taming It
subtitle: Controls, three lines of defence & a learning culture

hook.metaphor: A great kitchen isn't one that never has an accident — it's one where the burn gets caught early, the near-miss gets talked about, and tomorrow's shift runs a little safer because of it. Taming operational risk is less about heroics and more about layers of defence and a culture where people surface problems instead of hiding them.
hook.scenario: A staff member notices a control was skipped on a big payment — but it happened to be fine this time. In one firm they stay quiet (why invite trouble?). In another they report the near-miss and the gap gets fixed. Which firm just got safer?

learn: First, the **four basic responses** to any risk: **avoid** it (don't do the risky thing), **reduce** it (add controls), **transfer** it (insurance, or shift it to a third party), or **accept** it (consciously live with it within your appetite). Mature teams choose deliberately rather than defaulting to one.

Second, the organising idea of operational risk management: the **Three Lines of Defence.** The **first line** is the business that owns and runs the risk day-to-day. The **second line** is the risk and compliance function that sets standards and challenges the first. The **third line** is internal audit, independently checking that it all actually works. Each line is a backstop for the one before — and confusing the lines (e.g. the people doing the work also being the only ones checking it) is a classic failure.

Third, **controls come in flavours**, and you want a mix: **preventive** (stop the bad thing — limits, approvals, access controls), **detective** (catch it fast — reconciliations, monitoring, alerts), and **corrective** (recover well — backups, incident response). Prevention alone is brittle; you need detection and recovery for when prevention fails (it will).

But the quiet superpower is **culture** — specifically a **near-miss / no-blame culture.** Aviation transformed its safety record by treating every near-miss as free learning, reported without punishment. Finance is learning the same lesson: if surfacing a mistake gets you punished, people hide mistakes, and you lose your best early-warning system. This is 'tone at the top' (Governance Level 6) made concrete.
bridge: So at work, you can size up how seriously a team manages operational risk by looking for two things: a sensible mix of **preventive, detective, and corrective controls** across clear **lines of defence** — and a culture where people feel safe reporting near-misses. A firm that punishes the messenger is, whatever its policies say, flying blind.

interactions.title: The near-miss
interactions.prompt: A skipped control on a big payment turned out fine this time. What's the response that makes the firm safer?

options.label: Say nothing — no harm done.
options.feedback: A near-miss is a free warning. Staying quiet wastes it and guarantees the gap stays open until the day it *isn't* fine.

options.label: Report the near-miss so the control gap gets fixed.
options.feedback: Exactly the aviation lesson: near-misses reported without blame are pure learning. You fix the gap before it ever causes a loss.

options.label: Quietly punish whoever skipped the control.
options.feedback: Punishment drives reporting underground. Next time, people hide the miss — and you lose your early-warning system. Fix the process, protect the messenger.

interactions.title: Three lines of defence
interactions.prompt: Which line of defence does each role belong to?

items.label: The lending team running daily approvals
The risk & compliance function setting policy
Compliance challenging the business on a gap

interactions.title: Control types & responses
interactions.prompt: Match each term to its meaning.

pairs.right: Stops the bad thing happening (limits, approvals)
Shifting the risk elsewhere, e.g. via insurance
Consciously living with a risk within appetite

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “You tame operational risk with layers — a mix of preventive, detective and corrective controls across three lines of defence — and a no-blame culture where near-misses are reported, not hidden.”

checkpoint.q: In the Three Lines of Defence, internal audit is the…
checkpoint.explain: Third line = independent assurance that the first (business) and second (risk/compliance) are actually working.

checkpoint.q: A reconciliation that catches an error after it occurs is a… control.
checkpoint.explain: Detective controls spot problems quickly after they happen; preventive ones stop them up front.

checkpoint.q: Why is a no-blame, near-miss-reporting culture so valuable?
options: It lets people avoid all accountability
It turns near-misses into free early warnings instead of hidden gaps
It eliminates the need for any controls
checkpoint.explain: Punishing the messenger drives mistakes underground; safe reporting surfaces gaps before they cause losses.
